note:
当您走过任何一条历史悠久的街道时，都会看到标有日期的店面。美东时间。 1856年成立。 1912年。
 现代的观察企业成功的方式（立即扩大规模并最终获得收购）与一代代王朝家族企业的古老观念存在着一种令人发指的冲突，这种古老的观念伴随着他们几百年来的创始人，员工和客户的历史和DNA。 。
 拥有悠久历史，以核心价值为基础并忠于其原始价值观和传统的传奇公司，不仅因为其在错综复杂的时期内保持业务经营的能力，而且还因为他们能够适应并适应变化的能力而著称忠于自己和自己的宗旨。
 Sudo Honke成立于1141年，由Sudo家族的第五十五代经营，是其中之一。 879年以来，日本最古老的清酒酿酒厂通过大规模的社会，技术和经济变革而繁荣发展并幸存下来。最近，它抵御了福岛核电站产生的放射性污染的威胁，其产品仍保持原始配方的纯正性。
 Sudo Honke的总裁Gen-uemon Sudo先生意识到家人的坚定和坚强。
 “时尚一直在变化，但不会持久。对于我们来说，我们将继续制造传统的产品，这些年来吸引了许多人。”
 Sudo Honke创造了一种根植于他们自己的文化，当地特产和身份的东西。为此，他们开发了一种在全世界都广受喜爱和赞誉的产品。他们忠于自己的身份和所作所为，避免了已经摧毁了许多年轻创业公司的增长，规模和商品化。在这里，有一个教训是关于那些并非旨在一夜之间就烧光了的公司的持久机会。 
现在，当我们谈论成功的创始人时，我们很少谈论那些已经建立了长期业务并成功地从创业公司过渡到成熟，健康和繁荣的公司的人。在大多数情况下，我们谈论的是高增长，媒体和VC宠儿。理想不再是被驱使的创造者，他对工艺的执着使她能够设计和销售产品。作为个性的创始人，他出售了他们的产品，他们的公司，并最终出售了他们自己。
 我们发展了“良好成长”的心态。在我们的现代观点中，对于经得起时间考验的事物，没有什么是高尚的，美丽的或令人难忘的。如果发现它们阻碍了持续增长，那么无论它们是传统，价值观念还是古迹，都应予以销毁。这是一种古老而又全新的思维方式。保罗·贾维斯（Paul Jarvis）讲得很简洁。
 “从进化的角度来看，我们为什么要收集越来越多的原因是可以解释的：有了更多的食物，更多的水，更多的保护措施来防止掠食者，我们死亡的可能性就较小。但是今天，增长满足了我们的自我和社会地位。”
 人们对增长不再抱有什么希望，因为它不再具有积累更多增长的目的，即以投资者的报酬，公司金库和创始人的个人财富为形式。而且成本是天文数字。化石燃料公司永无止境的增长目标已注定了我们整个星球的所有意图和目的。诸如Google和Facebook之类的软件和平台巨头的不断增长，已经摧毁了人们的生活并破坏了民主本身。在较小的规模上，作为上帝价值观的增长已将有前途的创始人变成了烧毁的炮弹，追逐不可能的目标，并牺牲了他们的时间，生命和健康。
 Jurgen Appelo是《幸福管理》的作者。他的观点是，不受控制的增长不仅健康，而且具有破坏性。
 “为什么许多企业领导人想要更大的组织？如果一家公司的目的仅仅是增长，那么它就类似于癌症。您的业​​务应该努力做到最好，而不是最大。”
 在这种范式之外还有一个世界。只需要几个人，甚至只有一个创始人，就可以建立一个百万美元的公司。有些公司拥有增长的规模，而退出并不是成功的衡量标准。他们是拥有创始人激情，价值观和思想的公司，是每项决定和每项行动的核心。 
Basecamp是领先的生产力套件的创建者，有多本畅销书以及我偏爱的电子邮件客户端Hey，是这种成功企业方法的一个例子。
 “一切始于2003年。那时我们是一家网页设计公司。公司雇用我们重新设计和简化他们的网站。生意很好，我们很忙。但是我们杂乱无章。伴随着如此多的并发项目，事情开始悄然溜走。
 项目拖了太久。我们把重点放在了关键交付物上。我们之间存在一些严重的沟通不畅（“等等，谁说的？我们做到了？何时？在哪里？”）。
 那时，我们依靠电子邮件来处理一切。电子邮件在很多方面都很有用。但这对于长期运行的项目而言并不是很好。事情迷失了，人们被排除在对话之外，无处可去看看还有什么要做。明白我的意思了吗？
 因此，我们开始寻找项目管理工具。我们需要一些东西来帮助我们交流思想，组织要完成的工作以及向利益相关者展示工作。就那么简单。
 我们尝试了一些工具，但是它们很复杂且难以使用。因此，我们慢慢退回到使用旧的备用电子邮件地址。我们的问题还在继续。
 沮丧的是，我们决定构建自己的简单项目管理应用程序。几个月后，我们准备了一些东西。我们开始与现有客户一起使用此工具。 
立即项目运行得更好！我们重新获得了渴望的秩序和平静感。客户注意到–他们真的很感谢改进的沟通和组织。
 然后我们的客户开始询问我们我们正在使用什么软件来运行这些项目。原来他们想将其用于自己的内部项目！
 嘿，也许我们这里有产品！因此，我们对其进行了抛光，合理定价并将其投放市场。 2004年2月5日，大本营出生。
 我们在博客上宣布了它。一个月之内，我们就有大约一百个付费客户。数以百计的跟随。在不到一年的时间里，Basecamp为我们创造了比我们的网页设计业务更多的收入。我们很成功！因此，我们停止了设计网站的工作，全心投入软件业务。
 很快，我们开始听到有关Basecamp的故事，该故事已在学校，政府，教堂，咨询公司，出版商以及地球上几乎所有其他行业中使用。故事不断到来，我们不断成长。
 今天，距Basecamp首次面市22年后，已有超过2000万人与Basecamp合作开展了一个项目！每周都有成千上万的公司注册使用Basecamp。”
 该公司的建立，发展和运营均遵循创始人的步伐。他们从未进行过首次公开募股。他们从未对此表示任何兴趣。他们始终致力于文化，能力和宗旨，因此仍然是一家盈利公司。成为十亿美元公司的潜力一直存在。但它永远不会成为Basecamp的目标，就他们而言，它永远不会。 
这样一来，他们凭借难以置信的专注于客户和员工的力量，具有令人难以置信的低周转率，低间接费用，高收入，高利润以及对追求卓越的不断追求，从而使其与竞争对手和同行脱颖而出。增长发生了。但是增长不是上帝的价值。发生生长，但是生长不需要献血。
 作为天使投资人，公司避免过度增长的前景并没有吓到我。我对寻求收入和盈利能力的团队，产品和平台感兴趣，并且对可以在更长的时间内并通过其长寿有机地提供回报和收益的公司感兴趣。我想与具有坚实基础的创始人建立关系-建立持久的关系，没有利益冲突，同时将其视为人。
 我想起了《纽约时报》不丹研究中心主任卡玛·乌拉（Karma Ura）的这段话。
 “增长本身并不是目标，每个人都会同意。真正的目标是幸福和福祉，以及它们所代表的所有事物，例如活力与健康，社会同情关系，纯净而充满活力的自然以及意义与自由。增长与大多数目标之间没有必然的因果关系。因此，令人惊讶的是，作为一个主题的增长在全世界范围内的领导者中占有如此之高的地位。增长的“短期主义”既不鼓励我们将自己的一生都看向遥远的子孙后代，也不鼓励我们尊重我们赖以生存的后代的巨大成就。”
note_en:
When you walk down any street with a touch of history, you’ll see storefronts marked with dates. Est. 1856. Est. 1912.
 There’s a sobering clash of the modern way of viewing business success — immediate scale and ultimately acquisition — and the older idea of generational dynastic family businesses, carrying with them the histories and the DNA of their founders, employees and customers, dating back hundreds of years.
 Storied companies that hold a lo ng, rich past at their core and remain true to their original values and traditions are remarkable, not only for their ability to remain in business over staggering periods of time, but also for their ability to adapt and change while remaining true to themselves and their purpose.
 Founded in 1141 and run by the 55th generation of the Sudo family, Sudo Honke is one of these businesses. For 879 years, the oldest Sake brewery in Japan has thrived and survived through massive social, technological and economic change. Most recently it has weathered the threat of radioactive contamination from the Fukushima Nuclear Power Plant, with its products remaining pure to their original recipes.
 Sudo Honke’s president, Mr. Gen-uemon Sudo, is conscious of the solidity and strength of their family’s commitment.
 “Fashion changes all the time, but it does not last. For us, we will continue to make our traditional products which have appealed to many throughout these years.”
 Sudo Honke have created something that is deeply rooted in their own culture, their local produce and their identity; and in doing so, they have developed a product that is loved and celebrated throughout the world. They have stayed true to who they are and what they do, eschewing the growth, scale and commoditization that has destroyed many young startups. There is a lesson here about the lasting opportunities of companies that are not designed to burn bright and burn out overnight.
  When we talk about successful founders now, we’re rarely talking about the people who have built long term businesses, and made the successful transition from being a startup, to being an established, healthy and thriving company. For the most part, we’re talking about pump and dump high growth, media and VC darlings. The ideal is no longer the driven creator, whose dedication to her craft enabled her to design and sell a product. It’s the founder as a personality who has sold their product, their company, and ultimately themselves.
 We have developed a “growth as a good” mentality. There is nothing, in our modern viewpoint, that is noble, beautiful or memorable about things that are built to stand the test of time; if they’re found to be in the way of constant growth, they should be destroyed, whether they’re traditions, values or heritage sites. It’s a way of thinking that is both ancient and entirely new. Paul Jarvis puts it quite succinctly;
 “From an evolutionary point of view it is explainable why we wanted to gather more and more: with more food, more water, more protection against predators, we may be less likely to die. But today, growth feeds our ego and social standing.”
 Growth, where it no longer serves a purpose beyond the accumulation of more growth — in the form of investors’ returns, company coffers and the personal wealth of founders — is worshipped. And the cost is astronomical. The endless growth goals of fossil fuel companies has for all intents and purposes doomed our entire planet. The endless growth of software and platform giants like Google and Facebook has destroyed lives and damaged democracy itself. On a smaller scale, growth as a god-value has turned promising founders into burned out shells, chasing impossible goals and sacrificing their time and their lives and their health in the process.
 Jurgen Appelo is the author of Managing for Happiness. His view is that growth unchecked becomes not only healthy, it becomes destructive.
 “Why do many business leaders want bigger organizations? If the purpose of a company is merely growth, it is similar to cancer. Your business should try to be the best, not the biggest.”
 There is a world outside of this paradigm. It is possible to build a million dollar company with just a handful of people, or even one single founder. There are companies with growth scale and exits are not the measures of success. They’re companies with the founders’ passion, values and ideas are kept at the heart of every decision, and every action.
  Basecamp, the creators of the leading productivity suite, multiple bestselling books and my preferred email client Hey, are an example of this approach to business success.
 “It all started back in 2003. Back then we were a web design firm. Companies hired us to re-design and simplify their website. Business was great and we were busy. But we were disorganized. With so many concurrent projects, things began to slip through the cracks.
 Projects dragged on too long. We dropped the ball on key deliverables. We had some major miscommunication (“Wait, who said that? We did? When? Where?”).
 Back then, we relied on email for everything. Email’s great for many things. But it’s not great for long-running projects. Things get lost, people get left out of conversations, there’s nowhere to go to see what’s left to do. Know what I mean?
 So we started looking for a project management tool. We needed something to help us communicate ideas, organize the work to be done, and present work to stakeholders. Simple as that.
 We tried a few tools, but they were complicated and too hard to use. So we slowly slipped back to using our old standby — email. Our problems continued.
 Frustrated, we decided to build our own simple project management app. A few months later we had something ready. We started using this tool with our existing clients.
 Immediately projects ran better! We regained the sense of order and calmness we’d been craving. And clients noticed — they really appreciated the improved communication and organization.
 Then our clients started asking us what software we were using to run these projects. Turns out they wanted to use it for their own in-house projects!
 Hey, maybe we’ve got a product here! So we polished it up, priced it fairly, and put it on the market. On February 5th, 2004, Basecamp was born.
 We announced it on our blog. Within a month, we had about a hundred paying customers. Hundreds more followed. Within a year, Basecamp was generating more income for us than our web design business. We had a hit! So we stopped designing web sites and went all-in on our software business.
 Soon we started hearing stories of Basecamp being used in schools, governments, churches, consulting firms, publishers, and just about every other industry on earth. The stories keep coming and we keep growing.
 Today, 22 years after Basecamp first hit the market, over 20,000,000 people have worked on a project with Basecamp! And every week, thousands of companies sign up to use Basecamp.”
 The company has been built, grown and run at its founders’ own pace. They have never IPO’d. They have never expressed any interest in doing so. They remain a profitable company with their sights set firmly on culture, capacity and purpose. The potential to become a billion dollar company has always been there; but it’s never become Basecamp’s goal, and as far as they’re concerned, it never will.
 In doing so, they have an incredibly low turnover rate, low overheads, high revenues, high profits and an ongoing pursuit of excellence that sets them apart from their competitors and peers, by sheer weight of their dedicated customer and employee focus. Growth happens. But growth is not the god-value. Growth happens, but growth does not require blood sacrifice.
 As an angel investor, the prospect of companies avoiding hyper growth doesn’t scare me. I’m interested in teams, products and platforms that seek out revenue and profitability, and I’m interested in companies that can provide returns and gains organically, over an extended period of time, and through their longevity. I want to build relationships with founders that have a solid foundation — relationships that are built to last, without conflicts of interest, while treating them as human beings.
 I’m reminded of this quote from Karma Ura, the director of the Center for Bhutan Studies, in the New York Times.
 “Growth is not a goal by itself, everyone would agree. The real goal is happiness and well-being and all those things that they represent like vitality and health, socially empathetic relations, pure and vibrant nature, and meaning and freedom. There is no necessary causal or correlational link between growth and most of these goals. So it is surprising that growth as a topic has had such a hold on leaders all around the world. The “short-termism” of growth neither encourages us to look beyond our lifetimes to the distant future generations nor to pay our respect to the tremendous works of past generations on whose inheritance we live.”
title: 如果一家公司的目的仅仅是增长，那就类似于癌症
title_en: If the purpose of a company is merely growth, it is similar to cancer

note:
欢迎来到《火箭报告》 3.31版！本周的大多数新闻都发生在更大的火箭上，其中特别值得一提的是猎鹰重型火箭和NASA的Europa Clipper，值得一看。很高兴看到NASA和波音公司将Starliner的第二次测试飞行的发射日期提前了。
 与往常一样，我们欢迎读者提交文章，如果您不想错过任何问题，请使用下面的框进行订阅（该表格不会出现在启用AMP的网站上）。每份报告将包括有关中，小型和重型火箭的信息，以及日历上接下来的三场发射的快速展望。
  萤火虫寻求筹集更多资金。据《太空新闻》报道，萤火虫航空航天公司（Firefly Aerospace）本周表示，它希望筹集3.5亿美元，以扩大生产规模并开发新的更大的飞行器。 Firefly首席执行官汤姆·马库西奇（Tom Markusic）在周二的网络研讨会上谈到了太空产业的投资时说，这笔资金将支持其长期增长，因为它将Alpha火箭投入使用，同时该公司正在开发一种名为Beta的中型运载火箭。
 押注增长...＆＃34;在未来五年中，＆＃34;他说，“我们希望在我们外出飞行Alpha和SUV时，从一家市值10亿美元的公司手中拿走Firefly，到大约五年后，将公司的市值提高到100亿美元。” Markusic所指的SUV是一种称为太空多用途车的上层平台，可以用作太空拖船。马库西奇说，阿尔法火箭将在几周内首次亮相，而贝塔最早可在2024年首次亮相。 （由BH和Ken the Bin提交）
 Virgin Orbit获得商业发射合同。在首次发射成功的LauncherOne火箭之后，维珍轨道公司（Virgin Orbit）周一宣布，已被荷兰太空工程公司Innovative Solutions选择在太空中发射荷兰皇家空军的首枚卫星6U CubeSat称为BRIK-II。
 乘车共享任务……BRIK-II目前计划于2021年发射，将作为乘车有效载荷飞行，即将进行的LauncherOne任务。维珍轨道公司正在通过其子公司VOX Space从美国国防部的太空测试计划向该任务添加有效载荷。该任务将从莫哈韦航空和在加利福尼亚的太空港。 （由NotYourUsername，platykurtic和Ken the Bin提交）
  Starliner的发射日期向左移动。美国宇航局本周表示，它和波音公司现在的目标是不早于3月25日（星期四）启动Starliner的第二次非飞行飞行试验，这是该机构商业乘员计划的一部分。这比NASA宣布的前一天提前了四天，这是由于Atlas V火箭的可用性不断变化。 
下一次火箭升空……同样在本周，联合发射联盟宣布已推迟向美国太空部队发射STP-3任务的时间，以使客户能够评估STP Satellite-6的发射准备情况。航天器。该任务原定于Starliner之前发射，但现在延期意味着ULA可以专注于波音的试飞。 （由Ken the Bin和platykurtic提交）
 机组人员是首次首次私人发射的全名。第一次完全私人轨道太空飞行的机组人员将包括第二名进入太空的人，第二名以色列人进入太空，第11名加拿大人飞入太空，以及第一位前NASA宇航员返回国际空间站， CollectSpace报告。休斯顿的Axiom Space正在组织此次任务。
 预计在一年内发射。预定在SpaceX Dragon太空船上发射的是：美国房地产和技术企业家拉里·康纳（Larry Connor）； Eytan Stibbe，商人，前以色列战斗机飞行员；加拿大投资者和慈善家Mark Pathy；和已退休的NASA宇航员Michael Lopez-Alegria。该Ax-1任务可能会在2022年1月开始发射。（由Ken the Bin和Tfarog04提交）
 跟上Eric Berger空间报道的最简单方法是订阅他的新闻通讯，我们将在您的收件箱中收集他的故事。
 日本进入了H3火箭测试的下一阶段。该国下一代发射器H3的核心阶段将被运送到种子岛太空中心的发射场，从2月开始进行一系列测试。 《太空新闻》报道说，计划在三月份进行一次湿衣服排练，届时发射器将装有低温推进剂，以模拟发射前的工作。
 尚无发射日期……这些是最初有望在2020年发射的火箭的希望信号，但该火箭必须克服其新型LE-9主发动机的问题。但是，尚未选择目标发布日期（或至少已公开）。 JAXA表示，第一架H3火箭的发射日期将在与机载卫星及相关组织的发展状况进行协调后确定。 （由Ken the Bin和platykurtic提交）
 猎鹰9号发射的卫星数量创历史新高。周日，SpaceX乘坐猎鹰9号火箭发射了第一个专门的共享飞行任务，名为Transporter-1。据Ars报道，SpaceX通过此次发射总共143颗卫星，超越了印度单极卫星运载火箭在2017年设定的一次任务中发射的大多数卫星的纪录保持者。 
它有多受欢迎？ ... SpaceX尚未透露有关其乘车共享程序的普及程度或未来发射中预定的有效载荷数量的许多细节。但是，一些客户表示，他们对SpaceX提供的服务的成本和速度感到惊讶。该公司计划每年在Falcon 9火箭上执行几个这样的拼车任务。
  美国国家航空航天局（NASA）向Clipper商业发射征集信息。美国国家航空航天局（NASA）已发布了一项商用运载火箭的招标书，该运载火箭将其Clipper航天器发送到木星的卫星Europa。 ＆＃34;运载火箭应运载至少6065公斤的具有火星-地球-重力辅助弹道特征的欧罗巴快船，＆＃34;征集状态。 SpaceX的Falcon Heavy火箭是唯一具有这种弹道的商用火箭。如果要使用Delta IV重型助推器，则需要执行维纳斯飞越，这需要为Clipper提供额外的热保护。
    那么，为什么不将合同授予SpaceX呢？ ……多年来，国会授权快船使用太空发射系统火箭进行发射。但是，最近的风洞测试发现，SLS助推器会引起很高的扭转振动，并且有必要对航天器进行实质性的返工。因此，国会同意允许任务转移到私人火箭上。但是，由于国会中的某些人不是SpaceX的最大拥护者，他们希望NASA向尚未飞行的火箭开放竞争，其中包括Vulcan和New Glenn。在回应Twitter上的一个问题时，联合发射联盟的托里·布鲁诺（Tory Bruno）拒绝透露基本型号的瓦肯（Vulcan）是否能够完成NASA想要的飞行轨迹。 （由Ken the Bin提交）
 特朗普给拜登留下了火箭难题。那时，副总裁迈克·彭斯（Mike Pence）向美国宇航局（NASA）的SLS工程师表示了深切的关爱，已经过去了两年：如果美国工业界能够在没有政府发展的情况下提供关键的商业服务，那么我们将予以购买。而且，如果商用火箭是未来五年内将美国宇航员送上月球的唯一途径，那么它将是商用火箭。＆＃34;
 好吧，也许不是。。。今天，这位前副总统雄心勃勃的2024年登陆月球的目标已无法实现。便士已离开办公室。当然，SLS火箭没有在2020年发射。现在，几乎可以肯定的是，它不会在2022年之前发射。那么接下来会发生什么呢？这篇Ars文章着眼于NASA和正在建造的将人类送回月球的SLS火箭可能会发生什么。
 SpaceX擦洗了星期四的星舰发射尝试。 SpaceX一直在加紧准备在周四发射SN9原型机，但在当地时间上午11点之前，即“临时飞行限制”。当天的星舰发射被取消。在进行擦洗之前，工程师和技术人员一直在为发射做准备。当地居民也被疏散。
 Elon不高兴...发射似乎已推迟，因为FAA没有为10 km发射尝试授予最终批准。 SpaceX创始人埃隆·马斯克（Elon Musk）关于美国联邦航空局（FAA）发了推文，称其规则旨在每年从一些政府机构进行少量消耗性发射。在这些规则下，人类将永远不会进入火星。周五也有临时飞行限制，现在这是车辆最早可以飞行的时间。周五SN9的天气应该更加温和。 
太空部队与诺斯罗普的“蓝色起源”结盟。据《太空新闻》报道，到2020年底，美国太空部队正式终止了与Blue Origin和Northrop Grumman于2018年10月签署的发射技术合作伙伴关系。这是六年的公私合作伙伴关系，政府和承包商都同意投资于发展国家安全太空发射计划所需的火箭开发和基础设施。
 没什么吗？ ...与Northrop和Blue Origin的交易被终止，因为没有为该计划选择任何人。从2018年10月到2020年12月，Blue Origin的收入为2.555亿美元。最初的六年协议价值5亿美元。诺斯罗普·格鲁曼公司在同一时期获得了5.317亿美元的收入，几乎是LSA 7.92亿美元总价值的三分之二。作为投资的回报，太空部队将获得根据协议开发的公司的数据和硬件的有限权利。
 诺斯罗普进行火箭发动机验证测试。 1月21日，诺斯罗普·格鲁曼公司（Northrop Grumman）表示，已在犹他州的海角进行了对直径63英寸的加长石墨环氧树脂电动机的验证性地面测试。公司的GEM 63捆绑式助推器的这种变体是与联合发射联盟合作开发的，目的是为Vulcan Centaur火箭提供额外的升空能力。
 为火箭做好准备... GEM 63XL发动机发射了大约90秒，产生了将近44.9万磅的推力，以验证该发动机设计的性能。此外，试验点火还验证了在热环境中电动机的内部绝缘，推进剂颗粒，弹道和喷嘴。 （由Ken the Bin提交）
    2月20日：Antares |诺斯罗普·格鲁曼公司15 ISS供应任务|弗吉尼亚沃洛普斯岛| 17:36 UTC
note_en:
Welcome to Edition 3.31 of the Rocket Report! Most of the news this week has happened with bigger rockets, with an especially interesting bit concerning the Falcon Heavy rocket and NASA's Europa Clipper that's worth checking out. It's also exciting to see NASA and Boeing move the launch date for Starliner's second test flight  forward.
 As always, we  welcome reader submissions, and if you don't want to miss an issue, please subscribe using the box below (the form will not appear on AMP-enabled versions of the site). Each report will include information on small-, medium-, and heavy-lift rockets as well as a quick look ahead at the next three launches on the calendar.
  Firefly seeks to raise additional funding. Firefly Aerospace said this week it is looking to raise $350 million to scale up production and work on a new, larger vehicle,  SpaceNews reports. Speaking during a webinar on Tuesday about investment in the space industry, Firefly CEO Tom Markusic said the funding would support its long-term growth as it brings the Alpha rocket into service while the company develops a medium-class launch vehicle named Beta.
 Betting on growth ... "In the next five years," he said, "we want to take Firefly from a $1 billion company when we go out and fly Alpha and the SUV to, in about five years, being on the order of a $10 billion company." The SUV to which Markusic was referring is an upper stage called the Space Utility Vehicle that can serve as a space tug. The Alpha rocket is due to make its debut in a few weeks, and Beta could make its debut as early as 2024, Markusic said. (submitted by BH and Ken the Bin)
 Virgin Orbit nabs commercial launch contract. Fresh off the first successful flight of its LauncherOne rocket,  Virgin Orbit announced on Monday that it has been selected by the Dutch space-engineering company Innovative Solutions in Space to launch the Royal Netherlands Air Force's first ever satellite, a 6U CubeSat called BRIK-II.
 A ride-share mission ... Currently scheduled to launch in 2021, BRIK-II will fly as a ride-share payload on an upcoming LauncherOne mission. Virgin Orbit is working via its subsidiary, VOX Space, to add payloads to this mission from the US Department of Defense's Space Test Program. The mission will fly from Mojave Air & Space Port in California. (submitted by NotYourUsername, platykurtic, and Ken the Bin)
  Starliner launch date moves left.  NASA said this week that it and Boeing are now targeting no earlier than Thursday, March 25, for the launch of Starliner's second uncrewed flight test as part of the agency's Commercial Crew Program. This is four days earlier than the previous date NASA announced, and it is due to the changing availability of an Atlas V rocket.
    Next rocket up ... Also this week, United Launch Alliance announced that it has delayed the launch of the STP-3 mission for the US Space Force "to enable the customer to evaluate the launch readiness of the STP Satellite-6 spacecraft." That mission had been due to launch before Starliner, but its delay now means ULA can focus on Boeing's test flight. (submitted by Ken the Bin and platykurtic)
 Crew named for first fully private orbital crew launch. The crew of the first entirely private orbital space mission will include the second-oldest person to launch into space, the second Israeli in space, the 11th Canadian to fly into space, and the first former NASA astronaut to return to the International Space Station,  CollectSpace reports. Houston-based Axiom Space is organizing the mission.
 Launch within a year ... Slated to launch on a SpaceX Dragon spacecraft are: Larry Connor, an American real estate and technology entrepreneur; Eytan Stibbe, a businessman and former Israeli fighter pilot; Mark Pathy, a Canadian investor and philanthropist; and Michael Lopez-Alegria, a retired NASA astronaut. This Ax-1 mission could launch as soon as January 2022. (submitted by Ken the Bin and Tfarog04)
 The easiest way to keep up with Eric Berger's space reporting is to sign up for his newsletter, we'll collect his stories in your inbox.
 Japan moves into next phase of H3 rocket testing. The core stage of the country's next-generation launcher, H3, will be transported to its launch site at Tanegashima Space center for a series of tests beginning in February. A wet dress rehearsal is planned for March, when the launcher will be loaded with cryogenic propellant, simulating the work leading up to a launch,  SpaceNews reports.
 No launch date yet ... These are hopeful signs for the rocket originally expected to launch in 2020, but which had to overcome problems with its new LE-9 main engine. However, no target launch date has yet been picked (or at least been made public). JAXA says the launch date of the first H3 rocket will be "decided after coordination with the development status of the onboard satellite and related organizations." (submitted by Ken the Bin and platykurtic)
 Falcon 9 launches record number of satellites. On Sunday, SpaceX launched its first dedicated ride-share mission, named Transporter-1, aboard a Falcon 9 rocket. With this launch of 143 total satellites, SpaceX surpassed the previous record holder for most satellites launched in a single mission, set by India's Polar Satellite Launch Vehicle in 2017,  Ars reports.
 How popular is it? ... SpaceX has not disclosed many details about the popularity of its ride-share program or the number of payloads booked on future launches. However, several customers have said they were surprised by the cost and speed of the service SpaceX offered. The company plans to launch a couple of these ride-share missions per year on the Falcon 9 rocket.
  NASA solicits information on Clipper commercial launch. NASA has posted a solicitation for a commercial launch vehicle to send its Clipper spacecraft to Jupiter's moon Europa. "The launch vehicle shall deliver a minimum 6,065 kg Europa Clipper spacecraft with Mars-Earth-Gravity-Assist trajectory characteristics,"  the solicitation states. SpaceX's Falcon Heavy rocket is the only available commercial rocket capable of this trajectory. Were the Delta IV Heavy booster to be used, it would need to perform a Venus flyby, necessitating additional thermal protection for Clipper.
    So why not just award the contract to SpaceX? ... For years, Congress mandated that Clipper launch on the Space Launch System rocket. However, recent wind tunnel tests found that the torsional vibration the SLS boosters would induce is very high and would necessitate a substantial rework of the spacecraft. So Congress agreed to allow the mission to move to a private rocket. However, because some in Congress are not the biggest fans of SpaceX, they wanted NASA to open up the competition to rockets not yet flying, including Vulcan and New Glenn. In response to a question on Twitter, United Launch Alliance's Tory Bruno  declined to say whether a base-model Vulcan could complete the trajectory NASA wants. (submitted by Ken the Bin)
 Trump leaves Biden with a rocket dilemma. Only two years have passed since then-Vice President Mike Pence offered this tough love for SLS engineers at NASA: "If American industry can provide critical commercial services without government development, then we'll buy them. And if commercial rockets are the only way to get American astronauts to the Moon in the next five years, then commercial rockets it will be."
 Well, maybe not ... Today, the former vice president's ambitious 2024 goal of landing on the Moon has fallen out of reach. Pence has left office. And of course, the SLS rocket did not launch in 2020. Now, it's virtually certain to not launch before 2022. So what comes next?  This Ars article looks at what may be coming for NASA and the SLS rocket it is building to send humans back to the Moon.
 SpaceX scrubs Thursday Starship launch attempt. SpaceX had been gearing up to launch its SN9 prototype Thursday, but a little before 11am local time, the "Temporary Flight Restrictions" for the day's Starship launch were canceled. Before the scrub, engineers and technicians had been preparing the vehicle for a launch. Local residents were also evacuated.
 Elon not happy ... The launch appears to have been delayed because the FAA did not grant final approval for the 10km launch attempt. SpaceX founder Elon Musk  tweeted about the FAA, saying, "Their rules are meant for a handful of expendable launches per year from a few government facilities. Under those rules, humanity will never get to Mars." Temporary Flight Restrictions  are also in place for Friday, which is now the earliest possible time the vehicle could take flight. Weather should be more benign for SN9 on Friday.
 Space Force ends deals with Northrop, Blue Origin. At the end of 2020, the US Space Force officially terminated launch-technology partnerships signed in October 2018 with Blue Origin and Northrop Grumman,  SpaceNews reports. These were six-year public-private partnerships where both the government and the contractors agreed to invest in rocket development and infrastructure required to compete in the National Security Space Launch program.
 Not a great deal? ... The deals with Northrop and Blue Origin were ended because neither was selected for the program. From October 2018 through December 2020, Blue Origin was paid $255.5 million. The original six-year agreement was worth $500 million. Northrop Grumman got $531.7 million over that same period, nearly two-thirds of the LSA's total value of $792 million. In return for the investment, the Space Force will get limited rights to data and hardware the companies developed under the agreements.
 Northrop conducts rocket-motor validation test. On January 21,  Northrop Grumman said it conducted a validation ground test of an extended length 63-inch-diameter Graphite Epoxy Motor in Promontory, Utah. This variation of the company's GEM 63 strap-on booster was developed in partnership with United Launch Alliance to provide additional lift capability to the Vulcan Centaur rocket.
 Ready for a rocket ... The GEM 63XL motor fired for approximately 90 seconds, producing nearly 449,000 pounds of thrust to validate the performance capability of the motor design. Additionally, the test firing verified the motor's internal insulation, propellant grain, ballistics, and nozzle in a hot-conditioned environment. (submitted by Ken the Bin)
    Feb. 20: Antares | Northrop Grumman-15 ISS Supply mission | Wallops Island, Virginia | 17:36 UTC
title: 火箭报告：美国联邦航空局（FAA）将拜登的重大火箭困境“星际飞船”停飞
title_en: Rocket Report: FAA grounds Starship, Biden’s big rocket dilemma
